summary: My father was the principal of Yamabuki grammar school in Yamabuki-City. It was there that he instilled in me the importance of education, even though we could not always afford it. Yamabuki was the place where my early childhood was rooted before our family faced many separations and changes.

Japan was my homeland where I spent my childhood and early youth, including living in places like Yamabuki and Nara. My father traveled extensively throughout Japan before settling in Nara, and it was in Japan that I received my education and began teaching. Even after moving to America, my ties to Japan remained strong, especially through family and cultural customs like arranged marriage in the early 1900s.

When I was about 12, I moved to Nara to join my stepmother and brother after my father remarried and settled there. Taking the train to Nara was a new and awe-inspiring experience for me, opening my eyes to the wider world beyond the mountains of my childhood. Although the promise of education was slow to be fulfilled, Nara was where my family reunited and where my journey toward formal schooling began.

At age 15, I entered Giei Gakko in Gose, Nara, where I studied intensively for four years and graduated with honors. This period was one of the most pleasant and memorable in my life, as I transitioned from student to teacher in the local elementary school. Gose was where I first truly connected with education and began my professional journey.

I arrived in the United States on September 19, 1915, leaving my daughter behind temporarily to join my husband who was pursuing education there. My first year in America was happy, as I was welcomed by my husband and new friends, and I was baptized into the Christian faith. However, the years ahead brought hardship, including separation from my husband and the challenges of racial antagonism, especially during World War II and the internment period.

Upon arriving in the United States, I met Yohei Tanaka at a welcome party; he had been baptized just a week before and kindly offered to take me to church. His family became a constant guardian for mine until his death in 1973. Yohei's kindness and support were a great comfort during my early days in America.

The Bible became central to my spiritual journey after I was introduced to it in America. Although I initially struggled to understand its meaning, a minister named Rev. Kiyama helped me grasp its true significance, especially through sermons like the one on John 3:16. My faith deepened over time, providing me comfort and direction through many hardships.

Rev. Kiyama was a minister from Japan who gave a sermon titled 'THE JOY OF BELIEF IN JESUS CHRIST' that profoundly changed my life. I met him through Mrs. Michiko Fujita, who encouraged me to attend church during a difficult emotional time. His message helped me realize the shallowness of my previous faith and inspired me to seek redemption and a deeper commitment.

Jesus Christ became the center of my renewed faith after hearing Rev. Kiyama's sermon about the joy of belief in Him. The scripture John 3:16, which speaks of God's love and eternal life through belief in Jesus, deeply moved me and led me to commit myself to a sincere Christian life. This faith sustained me through many trials in America.

The scripture John 3:16 was the foundation of a sermon by Rev. Kiyama that changed my life. It spoke of God's love for the world and the promise of eternal life through belief in His only son. This verse helped me understand the true meaning of faith and inspired me to seek redemption during a very difficult time.

Mr. Gammier was a French designer of considerable fame who took me on as an apprentice in America. Under his close guidance, I refined my skills in designing and sewing, and after two years he encouraged me to open my own shop. When he accepted a job in Southern California, his business in San Francisco ended, but his mentorship was invaluable to my career development.

Southern California was where Mr. Gammier accepted a position in the motion picture industry, which led to the closure of his San Francisco business. Although I considered opening my own shop after his departure, I decided against it due to the strong racial antagonism toward Japanese residents in the area. Southern California represented both opportunity and challenge in my professional life.

After deciding not to open my own shop, I accepted a position as a table manager with Madame Alma. Despite warnings that she was difficult, I found her to be hardworking, dedicated, and warm, and we developed a relationship of mutual respect and friendship. Working with Madame Alma was an important and positive chapter in my life.

Dr. Gett Har was a young dentist I fell deeply in love with during the 1920s, even though his future was uncertain at the time. With support from friends and the wise counsel of Dr. Sturge, we finalized our marriage arrangement. His quiet dignity and compassion left a lasting impact on me until his passing in 1952.

After World War II, my husband and I found temporary lodging in the Sturge Building because our home had been lost and occupied by others. We shared living quarters with eight other families who had suffered similar fates. It was here that Mrs. Maruoka and I were appointed as the first women church leaders, a position we took great pride in.

Mrs. Michiko Fujita was a kind woman I met in a park during a very difficult time in my life. She noticed my distress and encouraged me to attend a sermon by Rev. Kiyama, which ultimately led to my spiritual rebirth. Her concern and support were pivotal in helping me find new faith and hope.

Dr. Sturge was a deeply inspiring and faithful man who provided guidance and protection to our church community. He conducted memorable Bible sessions, especially on Corinthians 13, and his dedication was evident in all he did. His sudden passing left a great void in my heart and in the church.

Corinthians 13 was the scripture Dr. Sturge meticulously reviewed during one of his memorable Bible sessions. The passage's message of love and faith deeply resonated with me and the church community. This session was one of his last before his passing, making it even more poignant.

World War II was a time of great hardship and racial intolerance for Japanese Americans like myself. In 1941, the war's outbreak led to the forced evacuation and internment of my family and community, shattering our hopes for fair treatment. Despite the sadness and disappointment, I did not harbor bitterness but was deeply tested in my faith and resilience.

During the Japanese American internment, my husband and I were first taken to Tanforan Assembly Center and later to Topaz War Relocation Center in Utah. The desert climate was harsh and the living conditions difficult, but I taught sewing classes there while my husband worked in the infirmary. This period was one of great trial but also of community and shared moments like admiring the brilliant sunsets together.

Tanforan Assembly Center was the first place where my husband and I were taken during the internment after the outbreak of World War II. It was a temporary holding site before we were relocated to Topaz in Utah. The experience marked the beginning of a difficult and uncertain chapter in our lives.

Topaz War Relocation Center in Utah was where my husband and I were relocated after Tanforan. The flat desert environment was a harsh change from the mild San Francisco climate. I taught sewing classes there, and my husband worked in the camp infirmary. Despite the hardships, we found moments of beauty, like gathering to admire the brilliant sunsets.

San Francisco was where I lived before the war and where I worked with Mr. Gammier and Madame Alma. It was also the city where racial antagonism influenced my career decisions. After the war, returning to San Francisco revealed that our home was lost and occupied by others, forcing us to live in shared quarters before renting a house and reopening my husband's practice.
The transcript does not provide specific information about Ransohoff's, so I do not have a personal story or connection to share about this organization.
